_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Objects practice 1
questions.prompt: Which uses the value in key?
choices: student[key]
student.key
student->key
student(key)
questions.explanation: Brackets evaluate the variable, while dot notation searches for a property literally named key.

questions.title: Objects practice 2
choices: the object is copied
a const error
questions.explanation: Both variables refer to one object, so the property change is visible through first.

questions.title: Trace Objects
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: Dot notation reads a fixed name, while brackets use the string stored in field. The resulting output is 'Ada\nCS'.

questions.title: Objects good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Objects?
choices: Use dots for fixed keys.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use dots for fixed keys. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Objects practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices:
const key = "credits";
console.log(student[key]);

const key = "credits";
console.log(student.key);
Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: Use brackets when the property name comes from a variable.

questions.title: Objects practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Trace this carefully'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Reading a missing property returns undefined; an error may occur later when code tries to use that absent value.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Reading a missing property returns undefined; an error may occur later when code tries to use that absent value.

questions.title: Objects practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Objects?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Use brackets for computed keys.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use brackets for computed keys. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Objects practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Objects?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Check optional properties.
questions.explanation: Check optional properties. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Objects practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Objects?
choices: Copy before independent mutation.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Copy before independent mutation. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Objects practice 10
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: Dot notation reads a fixed name, while brackets use the string stored in field. The output is 'Ada\nCS'.
